title: Why a tiny fee matters
intro: The expense ratio is the fee you never see on a bill. Here's why it still reshapes your corpus.

steps.title: A fee on everything, every year
steps.body:
The **Total Expense Ratio (TER)** is charged on your *whole* balance, *every* year, skimmed daily from the NAV — win or lose. You never get an invoice, which is exactly why it's easy to ignore.

Typical bands:

- Index fund: ~0.2–0.5%
- Direct active: ~0.8–1.2%
- Regular active: ~1.5–2.0%+

steps.title: Feel the compounding drag
steps.body: Because a fee lowers your growth **rate**, the shortfall compounds — it grows every year, just like returns do. Drag the sliders and watch how much a seemingly small % removes over decades.

steps.title: The free win: Direct plans
steps.body:
> [!tip] A fund's **Direct** plan is the *same portfolio* as its Regular plan, minus the distributor commission. Lower TER, faster-growing NAV, identical holdings.

If you don't need advice bundled into the product, the Direct plan is a risk-free saving that compounds for as long as you hold.

recap:
**TER compounds against you.**

- It's charged on the whole balance, every year.
- A 1% difference is not 1% of the story — it curves upward over time.
- Prefer **Direct** plans; compare TER *within* a category; don't chase the lowest number blindly.
